questions.question: Explain the load-store architecture of ARM processors with an example.
keyPoints: In load-store architecture, memory can only be accessed through specific instructions (LDR/STR)
Arithmetic and logical operations cannot directly access memory - they work only on registers
Example: To add memory value to register, first LDR value into register, then ADD registers, then STR result if needed

questions.question: List and explain the different privilege modes in ARM architecture.
keyPoints: User: Normal program execution mode
FIQ: Fast interrupt handling with dedicated registers
IRQ: Standard interrupt request handling
Supervisor: OS kernel mode for protected operations
Abort: Memory fault handling
Undefined: Undefined instruction handling
System: Privileged user mode with full access

questions.question: What is pipelining in ARM processors? Explain the 3-stage pipeline used in ARM7.
keyPoints: Pipelining is overlapping instruction execution in multiple stages
Improves instruction throughput by processing multiple instructions simultaneously
ARM7 uses 3 stages: Fetch (get instruction), Decode (interpret), Execute (process)
Each clock cycle completes one instruction stage, but one full instruction completes per cycle

questions.question: Explain the Thumb instruction set and its advantages.
keyPoints: Thumb is a 16-bit instruction set extension for ARM
Provides better code density (30-40% smaller code size)
Processors can switch between ARM (32-bit) and Thumb (16-bit) modes
Useful for memory-constrained embedded systems
Trade-off: Slightly reduced performance compared to full ARM mode

questions.question: Describe the CPSR register and its significance in ARM.
keyPoints: CPSR is Current Program Status Register holding processor state
Contains condition flags: N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (Overflow)
Interrupt disable bits: I (IRQ), F (FIQ)
Mode bits indicating current processor mode
Used for conditional execution and controlling processor operations

questions.question: Why are ARM processors preferred for embedded systems?
keyPoints: Low power consumption - essential for battery-operated devices
Small silicon die area - reduces cost
Scalable architecture - from simple to complex designs
High code density - reduces memory requirements
Flexible licensing - allows customization for different applications

questions.question: Explain conditional execution in ARM processors with an example.
keyPoints: Most ARM instructions can be conditionally executed based on CPSR flags
Instructions have 4-bit condition field (EQ, NE, GT, LT, etc.)
Eliminates need for branch instructions in many cases
Example: 'ADDEQ R0, R1, R2' executes ADD only if Z flag is set
Improves code density and reduces pipeline stalls

questions.question: Explain the RISC design philosophy and how ARM implements it. Also discuss the advantages of RISC architecture.
keyPoints: RISC philosophy: Simple, fixed-length instructions with uniform format
Load-store architecture - memory operations separated from processing
Large register file for reduced memory access
Reduced instruction set - fewer but more optimized instructions
ARM implementation: 32-bit fixed instructions, 16 general-purpose registers, barrel shifter
Advantages: Faster instruction execution, simpler hardware, better compiler optimization, lower power consumption
Disadvantages: Larger code size, more memory bandwidth needed

questions.question: Describe the ARM register organization. Explain the special registers and their purposes.
keyPoints: 16 general-purpose registers (R0-R13) plus PC and CPSR
R13/SP: Stack Pointer for stack operations
R14/LR: Link Register stores return address for subroutine calls
R15/PC: Program Counter points to next instruction
CPSR: Current Program Status Register with flags and mode bits
Banked registers in privileged modes: FIQ has dedicated R8-R12
SPSR: Saved Program Status Register saves CPSR during exceptions
Each mode has different register views for efficient context switching

questions.question: Compare FIQ and IRQ modes in ARM architecture. Why is FIQ considered 'fast'?
keyPoints: IRQ is standard interrupt request, FIQ is fast interrupt request
FIQ has dedicated banked registers (R8-R12) avoiding register save/restore
FIQ can disable IRQ during handling (priority handling)
FIQ vector located at end of vector table for efficient handling
FIQ has higher priority than IRQ in interrupt controller
Fast because: dedicated registers, simpler context save/restore, vector location
Used for time-critical interrupts like data transfer in embedded systems

questions.question: Explain the evolution of ARM architecture from ARM7 to Cortex series. What improvements were introduced?
keyPoints: ARM7 (1992): 3-stage pipeline, von Neumann architecture, used in early mobile phones
ARM9 (1999): 5-stage pipeline, Harvard architecture, better performance
ARM11 (2002): 8-stage pipeline, vector floating-point, improved multimedia
Cortex (2005): Thumb-2 instruction set, NEON SIMD, modern design
Cortex-A: Application processors for OS (Android, iOS)
Cortex-R: Real-time processors for embedded systems
Cortex-M: Microcontroller profile for MCUs,Thumb-only, energy efficient
Key improvements: Pipeline depth, cache, branch prediction, SIMD, power management
